label: Aztec Empire
pitch:
When Spanish conquistadors first saw Tenochtitlan, they thought they were dreaming — a gleaming island city of 200,000 people, larger than any city in Europe, fed by Chinampa Agriculture Expands floating gardens and connected by the Tenochtitlan Causeways to the mainland. The story begins with the Mexica Leave Aztlan on a mythic migration and reaches its climax when Tenochtitlan Founded in 1325 on the spot where an eagle perched on a cactus devouring a serpent. Then Cortes Arrives, and everything changes.

🔑 Key Facts:
• Itzcoatl forged the Triple Alliance Formed with Texcoco and Tlacopan, transforming the Mexica from vassals into the dominant power of central Mexico
• The Sun Stone Carved depicts the five cosmogonic suns and the Aztec calendar system in a single massive monument
• Nezahualcoyotl, the philosopher-king of Texcoco, composed renowned Nahuatl poetry (Nezahualcoyotl's Poetry) and designed the Chapultepec Aqueduct supplying fresh water to the island city
• Motecuzoma II presided over the empire at its zenith but faced the arrival of Cortes Arrives with 500 soldiers

🤔 Why It Matters: Aztec achievements in hydraulic engineering, agriculture, and urban planning supported a city larger than contemporary London or Paris. The Pochteca Trade Networks connected Mesoamerica from Guatemala to the American Southwest.

🎲 Wild Card: La Noche Triste — the Aztecs drove the Spanish out of Tenochtitlan in a devastating nighttime battle in 1520, killing hundreds of conquistadors weighed down by stolen gold. It was one of Spain's worst military defeats in the Americas, and it almost ended the conquest entirely.

spans.label: Mexica Migration
spans.description: The Mexica people journeyed south from their legendary homeland of Aztlan, guided by their patron god Huitzilopochtli. After over two centuries of wandering and temporary settlements, they arrived at Lake Texcoco to found their capital.

spans.label: Tepanec Vassalage
spans.description: After founding Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco, the Mexica served as vassals and mercenaries for the dominant Tepanec empire of Azcapotzalco. They built their city, established a royal dynasty, and gained military experience before revolting against their overlords.

spans.label: Triple Alliance
spans.description: Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan defeated the Tepanecs and formed the Triple Alliance, rapidly conquering the Valley of Mexico. Itzcoatl rewrote Mexica history, chinampa agriculture expanded, and the Templo Mayor grew as Tenochtitlan became the dominant partner.

spans.label: Imperial Expansion
spans.description: Under Ahuitzotl and Motecuzoma II the empire reached its maximum extent, stretching from the Gulf Coast to the Pacific. Tenochtitlan grew to over 200,000 people, larger than any contemporary European city, supported by long-distance trade networks and tribute from hundreds of conquered towns.

spans.label: Spanish Conquest
spans.description: Hernan Cortes and 500 soldiers, allied with thousands of indigenous enemies of the Aztecs, besieged and destroyed Tenochtitlan after a devastating smallpox epidemic. The fall of the capital in 1521 ended the Aztec Empire and began three centuries of Spanish colonial rule.

events.label: Mexica Leave Aztlan
events.description: The Mexica people began their legendary migration south from Aztlan, guided by their patron god Huitzilopochtli.

events.label: Settlement at Chapultepec
events.description: The Mexica temporarily settled at Chapultepec hill before being driven out by neighboring city-states.

events.label: Tenochtitlan Founded
events.description: The Mexica founded Tenochtitlan on an island in Lake Texcoco after seeing the prophesied eagle on a cactus devouring a serpent.

events.label: Tlatelolco Founded
events.description: A dissident Mexica faction founded Tlatelolco on a neighboring island, which became the empire's greatest marketplace.

events.label: Acamapichtli
events.description: First tlatoani of Tenochtitlan, Acamapichtli established the Mexica royal dynasty with claimed Toltec lineage.

events.label: Huitzilihuitl
events.description: Second tlatoani who strengthened ties with the Tepanec Empire through marriage to Tezozomoc's daughter.

events.label: Chimalpopoca
events.description: Third tlatoani who was assassinated during the Tepanec power struggle, precipitating the war against Azcapotzalco.

events.label: Tepanec War
events.description: The Mexica, allied with Texcoco and Tlacopan, defeated the Tepanec capital Azcapotzalco and ended Tepanec dominance in the Valley of Mexico.

events.label: Itzcoatl
events.description: Fourth tlatoani who forged the Triple Alliance and transformed the Mexica from vassals into the dominant power of central Mexico.

events.label: Burning of the Codices
events.description: Itzcoatl ordered the destruction of existing historical records so Mexica history could be rewritten to glorify the new ruling order.

events.label: Triple Alliance Formed
events.description: Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan formed the Aztec Triple Alliance, dividing tribute and territory among the three capitals.

events.label: Chinampa Agriculture Expands
events.description: Large-scale expansion of chinampas (floating gardens) in Lake Texcoco dramatically increased food production for the growing capital.

events.label: Motecuzoma I
events.description: Fifth tlatoani who greatly expanded the empire into the Gulf Coast and Oaxaca, and oversaw major construction in Tenochtitlan.

events.label: Templo Mayor Expanded
events.description: Motecuzoma I ordered a major expansion of the Templo Mayor, the twin-pyramid temple dedicated to Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc.

events.label: Great Famine of 1454
events.description: A devastating four-year famine struck central Mexico, intensifying Aztec reliance on military expansion and tribute collection.

events.label: Flower Wars Begin
events.description: Ritualized Flower Wars with Tlaxcala and other states were formalized to capture warriors for sacrifice and maintain military readiness.

events.label: Nezahualcoyotl's Poetry
events.description: The philosopher-king of Texcoco composed renowned Nahuatl poetry exploring themes of mortality, beauty, and the divine.

events.label: Chapultepec Aqueduct
events.description: Nezahualcoyotl designed the double-pipe aqueduct from Chapultepec springs to Tenochtitlan, supplying fresh water to the island city.

events.label: Axayacatl
events.description: Sixth tlatoani who conquered Tlatelolco and led an unsuccessful invasion of the Tarascan Empire.

events.label: Conquest of Tlatelolco
events.description: Axayacatl conquered the sister city of Tlatelolco, absorbing its famous market and unifying the island under Tenochtitlan's rule.

events.label: Sun Stone Carved
events.description: The famous Aztec Sun Stone (Calendar Stone) was carved, depicting the five cosmogonic suns and the Aztec calendar system.

events.label: Defeat by Tarascans
events.description: Axayacatl's army suffered a catastrophic defeat against the Tarascan Empire, one of the few powers the Aztecs never conquered.

events.label: Tizoc
events.description: Seventh tlatoani whose military failures and unpopularity likely led to his assassination by poisoning.

events.label: Ahuitzotl
events.description: Eighth tlatoani and the empire's greatest military conqueror, who expanded Aztec territory to its maximum extent.

events.label: Templo Mayor Rededication
events.description: Ahuitzotl rededicated the expanded Templo Mayor with a massive ceremony involving thousands of human sacrifices over four days.

events.label: Pochteca Trade Networks
events.description: The pochteca long-distance merchant guild reached peak influence, trading luxury goods from Guatemala to the American Southwest.

events.label: Tlatelolco Market at Peak
events.description: The Tlatelolco market served 60,000 daily visitors, making it one of the largest marketplaces in the world at the time.

events.label: Coyolxauhqui Stone
events.description: A massive circular relief of the dismembered moon goddess Coyolxauhqui was placed at the foot of the Templo Mayor.

events.label: Motecuzoma II
events.description: Ninth tlatoani who presided over the empire at its zenith but faced the arrival of Spanish conquistadors.

events.label: Tonalpohualli Calendar System
events.description: The Aztec dual calendar combined a 260-day ritual cycle (tonalpohualli) with a 365-day solar cycle (xiuhpohualli) to organize religious and civic life.

events.label: Aztec Herbal Medicine
events.description: Aztec physicians used over 1,200 medicinal plants, maintaining botanical gardens and a sophisticated medical tradition.

events.label: Nahuatl Flower-Songs
events.description: The tradition of flower-and-song (in xochitl in cuicatl) poetry flourished as the highest Aztec art form, performed at elite gatherings.

events.label: Codex Mendoza Tribute Lists
events.description: Detailed pictorial codices recorded tribute obligations from 371 conquered towns, documenting goods from cacao to eagle feathers.

events.label: Cortes Arrives
events.description: Hernan Cortes landed on the Gulf Coast with 500 soldiers and began his march toward Tenochtitlan.

events.label: La Malinche
events.description: Malintzin (La Malinche), a Nahua woman, became Cortes's interpreter and advisor, playing a pivotal role in the conquest.

events.label: Tlaxcala Allies with Cortes
events.description: The Tlaxcalans, longtime Aztec enemies, allied with Cortes, providing thousands of warriors for the march on Tenochtitlan.

events.label: Cholula Massacre
events.description: Spanish forces and Tlaxcalan allies killed thousands of Cholulans, sending a terror signal throughout the Aztec tributary network.

events.label: La Noche Triste
events.description: The Aztecs drove the Spanish out of Tenochtitlan in a devastating nighttime battle, killing hundreds of conquistadors.

events.label: Cuitlahuac
events.description: Tenth tlatoani who led the resistance during La Noche Triste but died of smallpox after only 80 days in power.

events.label: Smallpox Epidemic
events.description: A catastrophic smallpox epidemic killed an estimated 40% of the population of the Valley of Mexico, devastating Aztec defenses.

events.label: Cuauhtemoc
events.description: Last Aztec tlatoani who led the final defense of Tenochtitlan and became a symbol of indigenous resistance.

events.label: Fall of Tenochtitlan
events.description: After a 75-day siege, Cortes and his indigenous allies captured Tenochtitlan, ending the Aztec Empire and beginning Spanish colonial rule.

events.label: Templo Mayor First Construction
events.description: The original Templo Mayor was built at the center of Tenochtitlan shortly after the city's founding, dedicated to Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc.

events.label: Nezahualcoyotl
events.description: Philosopher-king of Texcoco who was a poet, architect, and lawgiver, and co-architect of the Triple Alliance alongside Itzcoatl.

events.label: Tlacaelel
events.description: The cihuacoatl (chief advisor) who shaped Aztec ideology, reforming the state religion to emphasize human sacrifice and rewriting Mexica history.

events.label: Great Flood of Tenochtitlan
events.description: A catastrophic flood inundated Tenochtitlan, prompting Nezahualcoyotl to design a massive dike across Lake Texcoco to protect the city.

events.label: Tezozomoc of Azcapotzalco
events.description: The powerful Tepanec ruler who dominated the Valley of Mexico for decades, making Tenochtitlan a vassal state until his death triggered the Tepanec War.

events.label: Cult of Huitzilopochtli
events.description: Worship of the war god Huitzilopochtli became the central state religion, driving the demand for captive warriors and ritual human sacrifice.

events.label: Tenochtitlan Causeways
events.description: Three great causeways connected the island city of Tenochtitlan to the mainland, featuring drawbridges and aqueducts that impressed the Spanish upon arrival.

events.label: Massacre at the Templo Mayor
events.description: Pedro de Alvarado ordered the massacre of unarmed Aztec nobles during the festival of Toxcatl at the Templo Mayor, sparking the uprising that led to La Noche Triste.

events.label: New Fire Ceremony
events.description: The Xiuhmolpilli (New Fire Ceremony) was held every 52 years at the end of the Aztec calendar round, when all hearths were extinguished and a new fire drilled on a sacrificial victim's chest to avert cosmic destruction.

events.label: Tlacaxipehualiztli Festival
events.description: The spring festival of Xipe Totec (Our Lord the Flayed One) involved priests wearing the flayed skins of sacrificial victims for 20 days, symbolizing agricultural renewal and the shedding of the old year.

events.label: Tlacaelel's Religious Reforms
events.description: Tlacaelel elevated Huitzilopochtli from a tribal war god to the supreme solar deity, reframing human sacrifice as cosmic necessity — the sun required human blood to rise each day.

events.label: Calmecac Priestly Schools
events.description: The calmecac (schools attached to temples) trained the sons of nobles and priests in religious ritual, astronomy, writing, law, and warfare, forming the empire's administrative and clerical class.

events.label: Toxcatl Festival of Tezcatlipoca
events.description: During Toxcatl, the fifth month of the solar calendar, a youth selected a year earlier lived as the living embodiment of Tezcatlipoca before being sacrificed atop the Templo Mayor.

events.label: Nezahualpilli
events.description: Philosopher-ruler of Texcoco who continued his father Nezahualcoyotl's traditions of scholarship and astronomy, and predicted catastrophic omens before the Spanish arrival.

events.label: Pochteca Expedition to Soconusco
events.description: Long-distance pochteca merchants opened the Soconusco (Xoconochco) cacao coast on the Pacific lowlands, bringing high-grade cacao — the Aztec luxury currency — into the tribute network.

events.label: Cacao as Currency
events.description: Cacao beans became the standard medium of small-scale exchange throughout the empire; a turkey cost 100 beans and a tamale cost 1, making cacao the everyday currency of market transactions.

events.label: Pachuca Obsidian Trade
events.description: Green obsidian from Pachuca (Hidalgo) was mined, worked into blades and points, and distributed across Mesoamerica via Aztec trade routes — the empire controlled access to one of the most prized tool materials on the continent.

events.label: Acuecuexatl Aqueduct
events.description: Ahuitzotl built a second aqueduct from Coyoacan springs (Acuecuexatl) to supply the growing population of Tenochtitlan, though its excess flow famously caused a catastrophic flood in 1499.

events.label: Codex Borbonicus
events.description: The Codex Borbonicus, a screenfold book produced by Aztec priests, recorded the complete 52-year calendar round, ritual almanac (tonalpohualli), and the New Fire Ceremony of 1507 in meticulous painted detail.

events.label: Telpochcalli Warrior Schools
events.description: Telpochcalli (houses of youth) trained commoner boys in martial combat, civic duty, and communal labor; graduates formed the bulk of the Aztec armies that sustained imperial expansion.

events.label: Gulf Coast and Oaxaca Campaigns
events.description: Ahuitzotl extended Aztec control to the Gulf Coast lowlands and deep into Oaxaca, doubling the empire's tribute base and bringing cacao, rubber, and tropical feathers into Tenochtitlan's markets.

events.label: Conquest of Huaxyacac
events.description: Motecuzoma I's forces conquered Huaxyacac (modern Oaxaca City) and the surrounding Mixtec and Zapotec towns, establishing a crucial southern tributary zone that supplied gold, cochineal, and jade.

events.label: Mexica Vassalage under Culhuacan
events.description: After being expelled from Chapultepec, the Mexica were taken in by the Culhua of Culhuacan as vassals and resettled at Tizapan — a barren, snake-infested land intended to destroy them. Instead they thrived, intermarried with the Culhua, and began absorbing Toltec cultural legitimacy.

events.label: Cocoxtli Grants Tizapan
events.description: The Culhua ruler Cocoxtli granted the wandering Mexica the desolate land of Tizapan on the southern shore of Lake Texcoco. What was meant as punishment became a foothold; the Mexica turned Tizapan into a settlement and began earning status as Culhua warriors.

events.label: Eagle on the Cactus Vision
events.description: Mexica priests reported seeing the prophesied sign from Huitzilopochtli — an eagle perched on a prickly pear cactus on a rocky island, devouring a serpent. This vision designated the founding site of Tenochtitlan and became the enduring symbol of the Mexican nation.

events.label: Defeat of Maxtla
events.description: Itzcoatl and his allies besieged and captured Azcapotzalco, killing the Tepanec tyrant Maxtla who had murdered Chimalpopoca. The defeat ended Tepanec supremacy and cleared the way for Mexica dominance — Maxtla's capture was celebrated with his sacrifice at the founding ceremony of the Triple Alliance.

events.label: Triple Alliance Tribute Division
events.description: The Triple Alliance formalized tribute distribution among its three capitals: Tenochtitlan received 2/5, Texcoco received 2/5, and Tlacopan (the junior partner) received 1/5. This arrangement made Tenochtitlan and Texcoco co-dominant and funded the construction of both cities.

events.label: Itzcoatl Conquers the Valley
events.description: Following the Tepanec War, Itzcoatl systematically conquered Xochimilco, Cuitlahuac, Mizquic, Coyoacan, and other towns around Lake Texcoco, consolidating Mexica control of the Valley of Mexico's heartland and establishing the tribute base that would fund all future expansion.

events.label: Motecuzoma I Conquers the Huastec
events.description: Motecuzoma I launched a major campaign into the Huastec region of the Gulf Coast after a rebellion against Aztec tribute collectors. The brutal reconquest extended Aztec control to the Gulf lowlands and brought cotton, rubber, and vanilla into the tributary network.

events.label: New Fire Ceremony 1403
events.description: The New Fire Ceremony (Xiuhmolpilli) of 1403 marked the end of a 52-year calendar round. All fires were extinguished across the Valley of Mexico, pregnant women and children were locked inside to prevent them from turning into monsters, and priests climbed Huixachtlan hill to drill new fire — if successful, the world would survive another cycle.

events.label: New Fire Ceremony 1455
events.description: The 1455 New Fire coincided with the devastating One Rabbit famine, making this ceremony especially fraught — the population feared cosmic destruction was imminent. The successful lighting of new fire was greeted with extraordinary relief and became associated with the beginning of renewed Aztec imperial expansion.

events.label: New Fire Ceremony 1507
events.description: The New Fire Ceremony of 1507 was the most elaborately documented, recorded in the Codex Borbonicus. Priests drilled new fire on the chest of a sacrificial victim atop Huixachtlan hill; when the flame caught, runners relayed fire to every temple and household hearth across the empire.

events.label: Tizoc Stone
events.description: Tizoc commissioned a massive circular sacrificial stone carved with 15 conquest scenes showing himself dragging captives by the hair — a propagandistic monument to military glory that his actual campaigns never achieved. The stone now sits in the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico City.

events.label: Ahuitzotl Reaches the Pacific
events.description: Ahuitzotl's armies conquered Tehuantepec and pushed to the Pacific coast of modern Chiapas and Guatemala, reaching the Xoconochco (Soconusco) cacao coast. This was the empire's furthest southern expansion and brought the finest cacao in Mesoamerica directly under Tenochtitlan's tribute system.

events.label: Coatlicue Statue
events.description: The eight-foot basalt statue of Coatlicue (She of the Serpent Skirt) — goddess of earth, life, death, and rebirth — was carved and installed in the sacred precinct of Tenochtitlan. One of the most powerful works of pre-Columbian sculpture, it depicts the goddess wearing a skirt of serpents and a necklace of human hearts and hands.

events.label: Calpulli Neighborhood Clans
events.description: Tenochtitlan was organized into calpulli (neighborhood clan groups), each with its own temple, school, land-holding, and governing council. The calpulli formed the basic unit of Aztec civic life — allocating agricultural land to macehualtin commoners, organizing labor levies, and maintaining local religious practice.

events.label: Macehualtin Commoner Class
events.description: The macehualtin (commoners) formed the vast majority of Aztec society, farming chinampa plots allocated through the calpulli and serving in the armies. Unlike European serfs, macehualtin could rise through military achievement — capturing four enemy warriors elevated a commoner to near-noble status.

events.label: Tlacotin (Aztec Slavery)
events.description: The tlacotin were Aztec slaves — not racial chattel but judicial and economic slaves who could be created by debt, crime, or self-sale during famine. Slaves could own property, purchase their freedom, and their children were born free. A sub-category, tecpan tlacotin, served in noble households.

events.label: Pochteca Merchant Courts
events.description: The pochteca guilds operated their own judicial courts, separate from standard Aztec law, to adjudicate commercial disputes among merchants. These courts had authority over matters of trade fraud, debt, and guild conduct — reflecting the semi-autonomous status of the merchant class within the imperial system.

events.label: Tlatocan Judicial System
events.description: Aztec civil and criminal justice was administered through a hierarchy of courts culminating in the tlatocan (supreme council). Judges were full-time specialists who heard cases daily; bribery of a judge was a capital offense. Nezahualcoyotl formalized this system in Texcoco and it spread throughout the empire.

events.label: Office of the Cihuacoatl
events.description: Itzcoatl created the office of cihuacoatl (snake woman — a title, not a gender), a vice-ruler who administered domestic affairs, supervised the judicial system, commanded the army in the tlatoani's absence, and managed tribute collection. Tlacaelel held the post for nearly sixty years, making him the empire's most influential figure.

events.label: Gladiatorial Sacrifice of Tlahuicole
events.description: The Tlaxcalan warrior Tlahuicole was captured by the Aztecs and offered his freedom — which he refused, demanding instead to die as a warrior. He was given the tlauauaniliztli (sacrificial gladiatorial combat): tied to a stone, armed with a feathered club, he fought eight jaguar and eagle warriors in succession before being sacrificed. He wounded several before falling, earning a warrior's death.

events.label: The Binding of the Years
events.description: The 52-year calendar round (xiuhmolpilli, meaning 'binding of the years') was the longest Aztec time cycle in common use — when both the 260-day ritual calendar and the 365-day solar calendar returned to their shared starting point. At each end-of-cycle, the Aztecs performed the New Fire Ceremony to ensure cosmic renewal.

events.label: Ticitl Healer Tradition
events.description: The ticitl (healers, both male and female) combined botanical medicine with ritual diagnosis, using the tonalpohualli calendar to determine which deity had caused an illness. They maintained specialized knowledge of over a thousand plant remedies, surgical techniques, and herbal anesthetics — a medical tradition documented after conquest in the Badianus Manuscript.

events.label: Badianus Manuscript
events.description: Written in 1552 by Aztec physician Martín de la Cruz and translated into Latin by Juan Badiano at the Colegio de Santa Cruz, the Badianus Manuscript documented pre-conquest Aztec herbal medicine with illustrated plant descriptions and treatments. It is the oldest known medical text from the Americas and preserves knowledge that predates the conquest by centuries.

events.label: Amoxtli Screenfold Books
events.description: Aztec scribes (tlacuiloque) produced amoxtli — screenfold books made from deer hide or bark paper (amatl) coated with white lime. These codices recorded ritual calendars, tribute lists, historical annals, and divinatory almanacs. Most were destroyed after the conquest; fewer than 20 pre-colonial pictorial manuscripts survive.

events.label: Cortés Lands at Veracruz
events.description: Hernán Cortés and approximately 530 soldiers landed on the Gulf Coast in April 1519 and founded the settlement of Villa Rica de la Vera Cruz, thereby establishing legal jurisdiction independent of Cuba's governor Diego Velázquez. Cortés then famously scuttled his ships to prevent retreat.

events.label: Cortés Meets Motecuzoma II
events.description: On November 8, 1519, Cortés and Motecuzoma II met on the causeway to Tenochtitlan in one of history's most consequential encounters. Motecuzoma received the Spaniards as honored guests and installed them in the palace of Axayacatl — a decision that would prove fatal to the empire.

events.label: Motecuzoma II Detained
events.description: Within weeks of arriving in Tenochtitlan, Cortés seized Motecuzoma II and held him as a hostage, ruling the empire through him. The tlatoani's apparent capitulation shattered his authority; Aztec nobles viewed his compliance as cowardice or supernatural obligation, fatally undermining resistance.

events.label: First Smallpox Wave 1520
events.description: Smallpox arrived in Mexico in 1520 aboard a Spanish relief fleet, spreading ahead of Cortés's forces. The disease killed tens of thousands including tlatoani Cuitlahuac after only 80 days in power — and had no name in Nahuatl because nothing like it had ever existed in the Americas. The epidemic destroyed military command structures and demoralized defenders at the worst possible moment.

events.label: Siege of Tenochtitlan
events.description: Beginning May 26, 1521, Cortés besieged Tenochtitlan with approximately 700 Spanish soldiers and over 100,000 indigenous allies — primarily Tlaxcalans, Texcocans, and Totonacs. For 75 days the Aztecs held out under Cuauhtémoc, fighting street by street as Spanish brigantines cut off canoe supply lines and smallpox raged inside the city.

events.label: Cuauhtémoc Captured
events.description: On August 13, 1521, the last tlatoani Cuauhtémoc was captured while attempting to escape across the lake in a canoe. The city surrendered the same day. Cortés later tortured Cuauhtémoc to reveal where the Aztec treasury was hidden, and executed him in 1525 during the Honduras expedition on charges of conspiracy.

events.label: Templo Mayor Phase II
events.description: The Templo Mayor underwent its second major construction phase during the reigns of Acamapichtli and Huitzilihuitl, when the original modest shrine was significantly enlarged with new stone facings and stairways — establishing the twin-staircase profile that would define all subsequent expansions.

events.label: Templo Mayor Phase IV
events.description: The fourth construction phase of the Templo Mayor, undertaken during Motecuzoma I's reign, was the most architecturally ambitious to that point — raising the twin pyramid to over 30 meters, adding the Coyolxauhqui stone at the base of the Huitzilopochtli stairway, and constructing the surrounding sacred precinct with new auxiliary temples.

events.label: Tenochtitlan Sacred Precinct
events.description: By 1500, the sacred precinct (Huey Teocalli) at the center of Tenochtitlan contained over 78 buildings including the Templo Mayor, temples to Quetzalcoatl and Tezcatlipoca, a tzompantli (skull rack) holding thousands of sacrificial skulls, a ball court, and the calmecac school — all enclosed within a massive serpent wall (coatepantli).

events.label: Quetzalcoatl Return Prophecy
events.description: The legend of Quetzalcoatl — the feathered serpent deity who had departed eastward across the sea, vowing to return — was interpreted by some (and later amplified by Spanish chroniclers) to explain Aztec hesitation toward Cortés. The year 1519 (One Reed in the Aztec calendar) was prophesied as the year of Quetzalcoatl's return.

events.label: Eight Omens Before the Conquest
events.description: Aztec sources record eight omens in the decade before the conquest: a comet, a spontaneously burning temple, a lightning strike without thunder, a weeping woman heard at night, a two-headed man, a bird with a mirror on its head showing warriors, and the lake boiling without fire. Whether invented post-hoc or genuine observations, they shaped how Aztecs remembered the conquest.

events.label: Florentine Codex Compiled
events.description: Fray Bernardino de Sahagún systematically interviewed Aztec elders from the 1540s through the 1570s to produce the Florentine Codex — a 12-volume encyclopedia of Aztec culture in Nahuatl and Spanish covering religion, natural history, economics, and the conquest itself. It remains the single most important source on pre-conquest Aztec civilization.

events.label: Nezahualcoyotl's Legal Code
events.description: Nezahualcoyotl of Texcoco established an 80-article legal code covering property, marriage, criminal offenses, and military conduct. Key provisions included death penalties for murder, adultery, theft by officials, and battlefield cowardice — making Texcoco one of the most legally sophisticated polities in Mesoamerica.

events.label: Albarradón of Nezahualcoyotl
events.description: After the catastrophic flood of 1449, Nezahualcoyotl designed and supervised construction of a 10-mile stone dike across Lake Texcoco — the largest hydraulic engineering project in pre-Columbian Americas. The dike separated the brackish eastern lake from the freshwater chinampa zone to the west, protecting Tenochtitlan from further flooding and enabling the chinampa agriculture that fed the empire's capital.

events.label: Ixtlilxochitl I of Texcoco
events.description: Ruler of Texcoco and father of Nezahualcoyotl, Ixtlilxochitl I was murdered by the Tepanec tyrant Tezozomoc when Nezahualcoyotl was a teenager. His assassination left his son as a fugitive, hiding in the mountains while plotting his return — the formative trauma that shaped the philosopher-king of Texcoco.

events.label: Moquihuix of Tlatelolco
events.description: Last independent tlatoani of Tlatelolco, Moquihuix attempted to build an anti-Tenochtitlan coalition among rival city-states. Axayacatl crushed the revolt in 1473; Moquihuix died in the assault on Tlatelolco's Templo Mayor, ending the sister city's political independence and uniting the two island capitals under Tenochtitlan's rule.

events.label: Nanahuatzin and the Fifth Sun
events.description: The Aztec creation myth placed at Teotihuacan: a humble, scabrous god named Nanahuatzin threw himself without hesitation into the sacrificial fire while the beautiful god Tecuciztecatl lost his nerve. Nanahuatzin was reborn as the sun, and the myth became the theological foundation for human sacrifice — the gods had sacrificed themselves to set the sun in motion, and humans owed the same debt to keep it moving.

events.label: Macuilxochitzin
events.description: Daughter of the cihuacoatl Tlacaelel and one of the most celebrated Nahuatl poets of the imperial period. Macuilxochitzin composed verse that was performed at elite gatherings and preserved in post-conquest manuscripts; she is among the few pre-Columbian women whose literary work is identified by name. Her surviving poems include accounts of Aztec military campaigns.

events.label: Battle of Otumba
events.description: In July 1520, two weeks after La Noche Triste, Cortés's depleted force of about 400 Spaniards and a few thousand Tlaxcalan auxiliaries met an Aztec army of roughly 40,000 on the plain of Otumba. Cortés's cavalry broke the Aztec center by killing the Aztec commander and capturing his battle standard — in Mesoamerican military convention, loss of the standard signaled defeat regardless of the actual numbers. The Aztec army withdrew, allowing the Spanish to reach Tlaxcala alive and regroup for the final siege.

events.label: Cantares Mexicanos
events.description: The Cantares Mexicanos is the largest surviving collection of pre-conquest and early colonial Nahuatl poetry, compiled in the mid-16th century. It preserves flower-songs, warrior hymns, and the great laments for the fall of Tenochtitlan — including the famous lines 'Broken spears lie in the roads / We have torn our hair in our grief.' Along with the Romances de los Señores de la Nueva España, it is the primary source for Aztec oral literary tradition.
